text: This video demonstrates early transanal closure after vacuum assisted drainage for anastomatic leakage in rectal cancer surgery.
This case involves a seventy-seven year old male patient presenting with a T-three-N-zero rectal tumor with a clear circumferential resection margin.
Patient did not receive neoadjuvant treatment and underwent a low anterior resection with a side-to-end anastomosis, six centimeters from the anal verge.
Patient did not receive a diverting ileostomy.
On postoperative day four, the patient developed increasing abdominal pain with high infectious parameters.
Abdominal CT scan showed an anastomatic leak from the circular anastomosis.
The patient was taken to the operating theater for a relaparoscopy and creation of a diverting ileostomy.
Endosponge treatment was started to control pelvic sepsis.
Endosponges were changed every three to four days.
Usually it only takes two to four endosponge changes to obtain a clean cavity, but this case took particularly long.
The presacral cavity is visible, showing clean granulation tissue, indicating readiness for closure.
The surgeon installs a transanal platform under general anesthesia following a perianal block, preparing for the next steps in the procedure.
The surgical view reveals the blind loop, afferent loop, and anastomotic defect within the rectal cavity.
A gauze is inserted into the afferent loop, visible within the surgical field.
This will be a control at a later stage to prevent accidental closing of the afferent loop with the transanal sutures.
The surgeon inspects the presacral cavity, which appears clean and filled with granulation tissue, indicating readiness for closure.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectal cuff from surrounding structures, addressing increased fibrosis due to multiple endosponge changes.
The surgeon places a six to eight French suction drain into the old abscess cavity before closing the anastomotic defect.
This is to prevent formation of a new abscess.
The surgeon inserts a suction drain one to two centimeters distal to the anastomotic defect, positioning it immediately posterior to the rectal wall.
The surgeon fixates the drain with a fast resorbable Ficryl-III-Zero suture after its placement near the rectal wall.
The surgeon removes and replaces the transanal platform, ensuring the drain remains outside the lumen to minimize interference during suture placement.
The drain is visibly positioned outside the lumen of the transanal platform and is securely fixated.
The surgeon closes the cavity using separate interrupted full thickness Ficryl-II-Zero sutures, ensuring secure closure of the tissue layers.
Gas is visible in the afferent loop, preventing complete closure of the defect by the sutures.
The surgeon removes the gas from the afferent loop after the complete closure of the defect, ensuring the lumen remains patent and uncompromised by the sutures.
Standard postoperative protocol entails drain removal on postoperative day five to seven and oral antibiotics for seven to ten days.
The surgeon checks the anastomotic integrity using an endoscope, visually inspecting the surgical site, followed by a CT scan with rectal contrast to assess the internal structures.
If both show a healed anastomosis, the diverting ileostomy can be closed.
The video shows the surgical technique for approximating the anastomotic defect following vacuum-assisted drainage, with visible sutures being placed to close the defect.
Thank you for watching.
